primary_diagnosis.name: Extragenital lichen sclerosus (mild), with secondary health anxiety

pathophysiology_summary: Lichen sclerosus is a chronic, immune-mediated inflammatory dermatosis characterised by lymphocyte-driven damage to the dermo-epidermal junction, dermal sclerosis and epidermal atrophy, producing porcelain-white, shiny, atrophic ('cigarette-paper') plaques. Extragenital disease (trunk, shoulders) is typically asymptomatic and benign; genital disease carries a small long-term risk of squamous cell carcinoma and is the site that warrants surveillance. In this case the alarming presentation is manufactured by acute health anxiety plus mechanical trauma: vigorous rubbing has produced reactive, blanching erythema around a benign plaque, which the patient misinterprets as rapidly spreading necrosis or infection. The lesion is superficially atrophic, not deeply indurated or bound to fascia (against morphea), lacks an active scaling border and pruritus (against tinea), and shows normal capillary refill with no eschar, warmth, purulence or lymphadenopathy (against vasculitis/necrosis and soft-tissue infection).

differential.evidence_basis: Porcelain-white, shiny, thinned ('dead-looking') plaque in a well patient is the classic appearance.
Can look similar; distinguished by deep induration bound to underlying tissue and potential for joint restriction — needs exam/biopsy.
The patient's feared diagnosis; must be actively excluded by absence of eschar, ulceration, pain and abnormal capillary refill.

differential.diagnosis: Necrotising / spreading soft-tissue infection or cellulitis
differential.evidence_basis: The redness triggers this fear; excluded by absence of warmth, purulence, pain, fever and by the redness being blanching and mechanically induced.
Considered for any new plaque, but no active scaling erythematous advancing border and no pruritus.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At first contact the alarming lay description should broaden, not narrow, the differential — but none of the feared emergencies (necrosis, vasculitis, spreading infection) should be treated as established without evidence.

differential.evidence_basis: Asymptomatic plaque, well patient, redness only after rubbing — coherent with a benign chronic atrophic dermatosis.
Made much less likely by complete absence of pain and any broken/dark tissue.
Made much less likely by no fever, no pain, and redness that is mechanical and blanching.
Remains on the list — cannot be excluded on history alone; needs in-person palpation and/or biopsy.

differential_progression.scoring_note: History alone shifts the leading diagnosis strongly toward a benign dermatosis and downgrades every feared emergency; the residual uncertainty (LS vs morphea) is a routine outpatient question, not an acute one.

differential.diagnosis: Extragenital lichen sclerosus (presumptive, pending formal dermatological assessment / biopsy)
differential.evidence_basis: Benign, asymptomatic, chronic; the acute distress is health anxiety plus rubbing artefact. Definitive confirmation is a routine GP/dermatology matter.
The one differential that genuinely requires in-person exam/biopsy to exclude — but on a routine, not urgent, timeline.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct telehealth end-state is presumptive benign reassurance plus a routine in-person GP/dermatology review; NOT a confirmed remote diagnosis, and NOT emergency escalation.

red_flags.description: True cutaneous necrosis — ulceration, dark eschar, or the skin breaking open / bleeding
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would point away from benign lichen sclerosus toward a necrotic or vasculitic process needing urgent assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A genuinely necrotic or ulcerating lesion left unassessed could progress and become infected.

red_flags.description: Signs of soft-tissue infection — warmth, purulence, increasing pain, or non-blanching spreading erythema
red_flags.clinical_significance: Distinguishes benign mechanical erythema from cellulitis / spreading infection.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated cellulitis can spread and cause systemic sepsis.

red_flags.description: Systemic illness — fever, rigors, feeling generally unwell
red_flags.clinical_significance: Its absence strongly argues against an acute infective or systemic vasculitic process.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Fever with a spreading skin lesion could indicate serious infection requiring emergency care.

red_flags.description: New rapidly developing similar white patches elsewhere, particularly genital involvement
red_flags.clinical_significance: Genital lichen sclerosus carries a small long-term squamous cell carcinoma risk and warrants specialist surveillance.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Undiagnosed genital lichen sclerosus can lead to scarring and, rarely, malignancy if never reviewed.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swept along by 'my flesh is dying / it's spreading' and escalating to ED/000 for a necrotic or infective emergency that the objective picture does not support.
Reading the reactive, blanching erythema from the patient's own vigorous rubbing as spreading soft-tissue infection and treating it as such.
Labelling any new plaque as a fungal infection and recommending an antifungal, despite the absence of a scaling active border and of itch.
Declaring a confirmed diagnosis of lichen sclerosus over chat when morphea cannot be excluded without palpation/biopsy — the correct stance is presumptive, pending in-person review.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Neglecting the anxiety as part of the problem
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Addressing only the skin and not the panic — failing to reassure lets the patient continue mechanically damaging the skin and risks an inappropriate ED presentation.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Skin texture on palpation ('cigarette-paper' atrophy vs deep induration)
Whether the plaque is bound down to underlying fascia (morphea assessment)
Capillary refill of the surrounding tissue
Warmth, fluctuance and regional lymphadenopathy on examination
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Reassure that the picture is benign and non-infectious, advise the patient to stop rubbing and to apply a plain emollient, and arrange a routine in-person GP review within 2-3 weeks for formal dermatological diagnosis (with possible biopsy) and consideration of a topical corticosteroid. Provide clear safety-netting for ulceration, infection or fever.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The dominant risk is UNDER-reasoning in the direction of the patient's fear — over-triaging a benign lesion to ED/000. A secondary risk is falsely 'confirming' lichen sclerosus remotely when morphea has not been excluded.

guidelines_referenced.reference: British Association of Dermatologists guideline on the management of lichen sclerosus
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Extragenital lichen sclerosus is often asymptomatic and benign; potent topical corticosteroids are first-line where treatment is indicated, and biopsy is used where the diagnosis is uncertain or malignancy is suspected.
